hook: Museum in the dungeons of the largest Nazi prison in occupied Poland, blown up in 1944.
facts: The Pawiak prison was built 1830-1835 to a design by Henryk Marconi, between today's Dzielna and Pawia streets.
During 1939-1944 it was the largest German prison in the General Government; roughly 100,000 people passed through, of whom about 37,000 were murdered and some 60,000 deported to camps.
In August 1944 the retreating Germans blew up the buildings; the museum opened on 28 November 1965 on the preserved underground foundations of cell blocks VII and VIII.
Since 1990 it has been a branch of the Museum of Independence (Muzeum Niepodległości).
A bronze cast of the prison's dead elm tree stands outside, hung with memorial plaques.

sources.note: Official museum listing, address ul. Dzielna 24/26, branch of Museum of Independence

w: The largest Nazi prison in occupied Poland stood here — some 100,000 passed through between 1939 and 1944; 37,000 never left. The Germans blew it up as they retreated; a museum opened in 1965 in the surviving cellar dungeons. Outside, a bronze cast of its dead elm hangs heavy with plaques. The Occupation, in the foundations.

hook: Polish Vodka Museum inside the restored 1897 rectification hall of the Koneser distillery.
facts: Opened to visitors on 12 June 2018 in the Centrum Praskie Koneser complex on Praga-Północ.

It occupies the historic rectification building of the former Warsaw Spirits Distillery (Warszawska Wytwórnia Wódek 'Koneser'), built in 1897; the facade still reads 'Rektyfikacja Spirytusu 1897-1970'.
The multimedia exhibition, designed by Mirosław Nizio, traces over 600 years of Polish vodka-making.
The ticket includes a voucher for a vodka tasting in the on-site academy bar.
The Koneser distillery operated on the site between Ząbkowska, Nieporęcka, Białostocka and Markowska streets until 1970.

sources.note: Official museum site, address plac Konesera 1, Praga

w: Six centuries of Polish vodka, told inside the 1897 rectification hall of Praga's old Koneser distillery — its facade still lettered 'Rektyfikacja Spirytusu.' The rooms trace the spirit from grain to glass; the ticket ends, fittingly, with a tasting. Distilling stopped here in 1970; the storytelling started in 2018. Na zdrowie.

hook: Tsarist political-prisoner pavilion in the Citadel where Piłsudski and Traugutt were held.
facts: The X Pavilion stands inside the Warsaw Citadel, a fortress built by Tsar Nicholas I after the November Uprising; from 5 July 1833 it served as the central investigative prison for political prisoners.
Inmates included Romuald Traugutt, Józef Piłsudski, Roman Dmowski, Father Piotr Ściegienny and Stefan Okrzeja; hundreds were executed on the fortress glacis and thousands deported to Siberia.
The original building was destroyed in a powder-magazine explosion on 13 October 1923.
The museum opened on 22 January 1963, on the centenary of the January Uprising, and is a branch of the Museum of Independence.
Its address is ul. Skazańców 25 ('Street of the Condemned').

sources.note: Prison history, notable inmates, 1923 explosion, 1963 opening, coordinates 52°16′00″N 21°00′01″E
Museum listing, branch of Museum of Independence, address ul. Skazańców 25 Żoliborz

w: The Tsar's interrogation prison inside the Warsaw Citadel, the fortress Nicholas I raised to cow the city after 1830 — its Tenth Pavilion held Traugutt, Piłsudski and Dmowski before the gallows on the glacis or the long road to Siberia. A museum since 1963, on a street named 'of the Condemned.' Where Poland's rebels waited.

hook: One of the world's largest Jewish cemeteries, ~200,000 matzevot under the trees of Wola.
facts: Established in 1806 by the Warsaw Jewish community at ul. Okopowa 49/51; the first burial took place on 6 December 1806.
It covers about 33.5 hectares and holds roughly 200,000 gravestones (matzevot), making it the largest Jewish cemetery in Mazovia and Poland's second-largest after Łódź.
Among those buried are Yiddish writer Icchok Lejb Perec, actress Ester Rachel Kamińska, Esperanto creator Ludwik Zamenhof, and Ghetto Uprising leader Marek Edelman.
It survived the war and remains active, with one or two burials a month in recent decades.
The cemetery is divided into orthodox, progressive, children's, military and ghetto quarters, with about 40 ohels over the graves of rabbis and tzadikim.

sources.note: Founding 1806, size 33.5 ha, ~200,000 graves, notable burials, coordinates 52°14′46″N 20°58′25″E
Wirtualny Sztetl listing, address Okopowa 49/51, Wola

w: One of the largest Jewish cemeteries on earth — thirty-three hectares and some 200,000 matzevot under the trees of Wola, opened in 1806 and never closed. Zamenhof, who invented Esperanto, lies here; so do the writer Perec and Marek Edelman of the Ghetto Uprising. Ohels over the tzadikim, ivy over everything. A whole world, in stone.

hook: A hands-on museum of 100-plus playable pinball and arcade machines on ul. Kolejowa.
facts: Warsaw's first interactive pinball museum, opened in June 2016 at ul. Kolejowa 8A in Wola.
The collection numbers over 100 (reported up to ~150) pinball and arcade machines, with around 60-100 restored and free to play on a single admission ticket.
Machines span decades of pinball history, from electromechanical tables to modern solid-state games.
It sits about 900 m from the Rondo Daszyńskiego metro station on the M2 line.

sources.note: Official site: first interactive pinball museum, opened June 2016, address Kolejowa 8A, collection size

w: A hundred-odd pinball and arcade machines, all restored and all playable on one ticket — decades of flipper history on ul. Kolejowa, from clattering electromechanical tables to modern solid-state. No 'do not touch' signs here; the whole point is to play. Warsaw's most hands-on museum. Bring loose wrists and a couple of hours.

hook: 1901 market hall turned food court and boxing arena — and a 1944 massacre site.
facts: Built 1899-1902 as the eastern twin of Hala Mirowska, Warsaw's first great market halls, on the site of the demolished Mirów Barracks at plac Żelaznej Bramy.
Each hall measured about 95 m long and 43 m wide, with a locally produced steel framework.
During the Warsaw Uprising in August 1944 the halls were a site of mass executions of civilians from western Śródmieście; the bodies were burned by a German Verbrennungskommando.
Postwar it became a sports venue, hosting the 1953 European Boxing Championships and 1959 World Weightlifting Championships.
It later reopened as a food-and-market hall and houses the Feliks Stamm boxing museum; in December 2025 the city signed a contract for a full restoration.

sources.note: Construction 1899-1902, dimensions, 1944 executions, sports use, coordinates 52°14′19″N 20°59′49″E
City of Warsaw on the hall's history and 2025 modernization

w: One of Warsaw's first great market halls, built in 1901 as the twin of Hala Mirowska on plac Żelaznej Bramy — and a place of horror in 1944, when the Germans shot Śródmieście's civilians here and burned the bodies. Afterwards a boxing arena; today a food hall with a boxing museum inside. Markets, then murder, now meatballs.

hook: Restored 1909 Art Nouveau market hall, demolished and rebuilt as a gourmet food court.
facts: Built 1906-1909 to a design by Juliusz Dzierżanowski in the then-fashionable Secesja (Art Nouveau) style; it opened on 2 March 1909.
It originally held 4 large two-storey shops, 24 smaller stores, 60 butcher stalls, 12 fish stalls and 144 market stands.
The facades carry sculptural decoration by Zygmunt Otto, including a heraldic cartouche with Warsaw's Syrenka (mermaid) and rare animal motifs such as an ox head over the gates.
Controversially demolished in 2009, it was reconstructed and reopened in October 2016 (JEMS Architekci) as a market-and-restaurant hall with restored steel framework.
It stands at ul. Koszykowa 61/63 in Śródmieście Południowe.

sources.note: Architect, 1906-1909, Art Nouveau, Syrenka relief, 2009 demolition / 2016 rebuild, coordinates 52°13′20″N 21°00′39″E
English corroboration of history, address and reopening

w: A 1909 Art Nouveau market hall, all curling steel and a mermaid carved over the gates — demolished, controversially, in 2009, then painstakingly rebuilt by 2016. Where butchers and fishmongers once kept 144 stalls, a buzzing food court now fills the restored frame. Secesja saved, after a fashion. Eat beneath the old ironwork.

hook: Halina Skibniewska's celebrated 1960s estate, low blocks woven between preserved orchard trees.
facts: Built 1958-1972 (first colony 1959-1964) on the WSM cooperative land in western Żoliborz, on the site of the former village of Buraków.
Designed by architect Halina Skibniewska, widely regarded as one of the finest housing estates of the PRL era.

Skibniewska deliberately preserved the existing fruit trees (hence 'sady', orchards), giving the estate its intimate, green character, and avoided large-panel prefab construction in favour of brick and wood.
The blocks have flexible interiors with no internal load-bearing walls; pedestrian and car traffic were separated, with semi-enclosed colonies and courtyards for children.
A building at ul. Krasińskiego 34a won the 'Mister Warszawy 1961' architecture prize.

sources.note: Skibniewska design, preserved fruit trees, boundaries, coordinates 52.267433, 20.970456
History of the estate, 1958-1972, Mister Warszawy 1961

w: Halina Skibniewska's 1960s housing estate, and proof that the PRL could build with a soul — low blocks of brick and wood threaded between the trees of an old orchard she refused to fell, hence 'sady'. No prefab panels, no load-bearing inner walls, cars kept from the courtyards where children played. Communist Poland at its most humane.
